title: Turn rough notes into a crisp 1-page business memo
content:
Convert my raw notes into a clear, professional one-page business memo.

Requirements:
- Keep it concise and skimmable (max ~400 words).
- Use plain language; no hype, no jargon.
- Preserve all facts and numbers exactly; don’t invent details.
- If anything is ambiguous or missing, list it under “Open Questions” instead of guessing.

Format:
1) Title
2) Context (2–3 sentences)
3) Key Points (5–7 bullets)
4) Recommendation (1 short paragraph)
5) Risks & Mitigations (3 bullets)
6) Next Steps (3 bullets with owner + due date placeholders)
7) Open Questions (bullets)

My notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Turn a Feature List into a High-Converting Landing Page (SaaS)
content:
Help me write landing page copy for my SaaS in a friendly, helpful tone. Keep it simple and conversion-focused.

Here’s the input:
- Product name: [NAME]
- Target customer: [WHO IT’S FOR]
- Problem they have: [PAIN]
- Desired outcome: [OUTCOME]
- Top 5 features (bullets):
  1) [FEATURE]
  2) [FEATURE]
  3) [FEATURE]
  4) [FEATURE]
  5) [FEATURE]
- Key differentiator: [WHY US]
- Proof available (optional): [TESTIMONIALS / METRICS / LOGOS]
- Primary CTA: [CTA]
- Secondary CTA (optional): [CTA]
- Any words to avoid: [LIST]

Write:
1) Hero section: headline (max 10 words) + subheadline (1–2 sentences) + primary CTA + a short trust line.
2) “How it works” section: 3 steps, each with a one-line title and 2–3 lines of explanation.
3) Benefits section: 6 benefit bullets written from the customer’s perspective (not feature repeats).
4) Feature highlights: pick the 3 most compelling features and write a short paragraph for each.
5) Social proof block: 2 testimonial examples (if none provided, write placeholders that don’t sound fake).
6) FAQ: 6 questions with concise answers; include pricing, setup time, integrations, security/privacy, and “is this for me?”.
7) Closing CTA section: a fresh headline + 2 lines of reassurance + primary CTA.

Constraints:
- Use plain language. No hype, no jargon.
- Assume the reader is skimming; keep paragraphs short.
- If anything is unclear, make reasonable assumptions and note them at the end under “Assumptions.”

title: Instagram carousel hero image for a growth marketing framework
content:
Create a square Instagram carousel cover (1:1) featuring a bold, modern “Growth Marketing Framework” hero graphic. Minimalist design with a clean grid layout and strong hierarchy.

Scene & composition:
- Centered title: “GROWTH MARKETING FRAMEWORK” in large, uppercase sans-serif
- Subheading beneath: “Acquire • Activate • Retain • Revenue • Referral”
- A subtle 5-step pipeline diagram across the middle (five connected blocks with small icons)
- Bottom-right small tag: “Playbook”
- Plenty of negative space; symmetrical alignment; crisp margins

Style & palette:
- Confident, structured, tech-forward aesthetic
- Background: off-white (#F6F4EF) with a faint grid texture
- Primary accent: deep navy (#0B1F3B)
- Secondary accents: electric blue (#2D7FF9) and lime (#A7F542) used sparingly for highlights

Typography & graphics:
- Typeface: modern geometric sans (similar to Inter / Neue Haas Grotesk)
- Clean vector icons (no cartoons): magnet (Acquire), spark (Activate), shield (Retain), chart (Revenue), megaphone (Referral)
- Thin line dividers, consistent stroke weight, precise spacing

Lighting & rendering:
- Flat design / vector look, ultra-sharp edges
- No photos, no 3D, no mockups, no drop shadows beyond a very subtle 2% shadow for depth

Output constraints:
- No watermarks, no extra logos, no clutter
- Text must be perfectly legible and correctly spelled
- High-resolution, print-ready clarity

title: Modern, direct outreach email that earns a reply (with 3 subject options)
content:
Write a modern, direct outreach email I can send to a potential customer.

Context
- Who I’m emailing: [job title + company + brief context]
- What they likely care about: [top 2 priorities]
- My offer: [what you sell in 1 sentence]
- Proof: [1 metric, result, or recognizable customer/logo—optional]
- CTA I want: [15-min call / quick reply / intro]
- Link (optional): [URL]
- My name + role: [signature]

Requirements
- Provide 3 subject lines (short, non-salesy, no emojis)
- Email body: 110–160 words
- Tone: modern and direct, confident but not pushy
- Personalize using the context above; don’t invent facts
- Make the value specific (not “increase efficiency”)
- Include one clear CTA question at the end
- Avoid: hype, buzzwords, excessive exclamation points, and “just following up” phrasing

Deliver
1) Subject line options
2) Email (plain text)
3) One optional alternate closing line (e.g., “Worth a look?”)

title: Turn a Business Idea into a 1-Page Execution Plan
content:
I’m considering this business idea:

[Paste your idea in 3–8 sentences]

Turn it into a one-page execution plan I can act on this week. Keep it clear, practical, and low-jargon. Include:

- Target customer: who they are, what they’re trying to accomplish, and what triggers them to look for a solution
- Problem & current alternatives: how they solve it today and why that’s frustrating/expensive/risky
- Value proposition: 1 sentence + 3 bullet benefits (outcomes, not features)
- Offer: what exactly I’m selling (package, pricing model options, what’s included/excluded)
- Acquisition: 3 realistic channels to test first, with a simple first experiment for each (what to do, what to measure)
- MVP scope: the smallest version that delivers the core result, plus what to deliberately NOT build yet
- First 10 customers plan: a step-by-step outreach plan for the next 7 days
- Key risks & assumptions: top 5, and a quick way to validate each
- Metrics: 5 numbers to track weekly (leading + lagging)
- 30/60/90-day milestones: short bullets with a “done means” definition

If anything essential is missing from my idea, ask up to 5 questions first. Otherwise, make reasonable assumptions and state them.

title: Turn a rough UI idea into a crisp PRD + wireframe checklist
content:
Take my rough product idea and turn it into a lightweight UI/UX spec I can hand to design and engineering.

Input (I’ll paste below):
- Product/context:
- Target users:
- Core job-to-be-done:
- Platform (web/iOS/Android):
- Key screens I think we need (optional):
- Constraints (timeline/tech/legal/brand):
- Success metric:

Output:
1) One-sentence problem statement
2) Assumptions + open questions (only the ones that materially change the design)
3) Primary user flows (3–7) written as: Trigger → Steps → Outcome
4) Screen inventory with purpose for each screen
5) For each screen: required components + key states (loading/empty/error/success)
6) Data requirements: what data is shown/created/edited and validations
7) Edge cases: top 8 that will break the experience if ignored
8) Accessibility and responsive considerations (short, actionable)
9) Analytics events: event name → when fired → key properties
10) Acceptance criteria: 8–12 bullet checks written as testable statements

Style rules:
- Be professional and concise.
- Prefer specifics over jargon.
- If details are missing, make a reasonable assumption and label it clearly.

Here’s the idea:
[PASTE IDEA]

title: Clean app growth dashboard illustration (hero image prompt)
content: Create an image: a modern SaaS “growth dashboard” hero illustration on a light background. Show a laptop screen with a clean analytics UI: line chart trending upward, bar chart by channel, small KPI cards (MRR, CAC, Conversion Rate), and a subtle grid pattern in the background. Add a few minimal floating UI elements (toggle, filter dropdown, calendar) to imply interactivity. Style: crisp vector + slight depth (soft shadows), rounded corners, friendly color palette (teal, indigo, coral accents), lots of whitespace. Composition: centered laptop, dashboard clearly readable, balanced negative space for headline text on the left. Lighting: soft, even, no harsh contrast. Mood: optimistic, calm, trustworthy. No people, no logos, no brand names, no watermark. High resolution, sharp edges, clean typography shapes (not actual readable text).

title: Futuristic coding workstation hero image (no overlay text, clean cover-ready)
content:
Create a cover-ready hero image for a coding-themed project with NO text, NO logos, NO watermarks, and plenty of clean negative space for a title added later.

Scene: a modern developer workstation in a dim, futuristic studio. A wide ultrawide monitor shows crisp code (generic syntax, not recognizable snippets), a terminal window, and subtle UI panels. Desk is matte black with tidy cable management, compact mechanical keyboard, trackpad, and a small notebook.

Composition: 16:9 landscape. Camera at a slight high angle, three-quarter view from the left. Subject centered-right, leaving a smooth dark gradient area on the left for later typography. Keep the frame uncluttered.

Lighting & mood: confident, high-clarity, cinematic. Cool teal and deep indigo ambient light with a warm rim light on the desk edge. Soft volumetric light, subtle bokeh, realistic reflections on the monitor, gentle haze.

Style: photorealistic, premium tech aesthetic, sharp details, clean lines, modern minimalism. High dynamic range, low noise, natural depth of field.

Background: abstract but believable studio backdrop with faint geometric patterns and a few out-of-focus LED strips; no brand shapes.

Negative prompts: text, captions, logos, watermarks, UI brand marks, messy desk, extra hands, faces, distorted keyboard, unreadable glitch text, overexposed highlights.

title: Daily Priority Triage: Pick the 3 Tasks That Actually Move the Needle
content:
I’m going to paste a list of tasks, messages, and loose ideas. Turn it into a simple plan for today.

Rules:
- Choose the Top 3 priorities based on impact and urgency.
- Flag anything that’s not worth doing today as “Defer” or “Drop” (be decisive).
- Keep it realistic for a normal workday.
- If something is unclear, make a reasonable assumption and note it.

Output format:
1) Top 3 (in order)
- Task:
- Why it matters (1 sentence):
- Next action (specific, <15 minutes):
- Time block suggestion:

2) Quick Wins (optional, max 5)
- Each: task + next action

3) Defer
- Task + when to revisit

4) Drop
- Task + short reason

Here’s my list:
[paste tasks here]

title: Deep Research Prompt: Evaluate a Market Opportunity and Produce an Evidence-Backed POV
content:
Help me evaluate a market opportunity with rigorous, source-aware research and produce an actionable point of view.

Context
- Topic / market: [PASTE]
- Target customer: [PASTE]
- Geography: [PASTE]
- Time horizon (now / 12–24 months): [PASTE]
- My starting hypothesis (if any): [PASTE]
- What a “good answer” helps me decide: [PASTE]

Requirements
- Be clear, practical, and skeptical. Prefer primary sources, official datasets, and credible industry research.
- If you are unsure, say so and propose how to verify.
- Use explicit assumptions; separate facts vs. inference.
- No fluff, no motivational language.

First, ask up to 7 high-leverage clarifying questions only if the answers would materially change the conclusion. Otherwise proceed and state assumptions.

Deliverables (use headings exactly)
1) Executive POV (10 bullets max)
- My best current conclusion, plus the single biggest “make-or-break” uncertainty.

2) Market Definition
- Define the market precisely (what’s in/out).
- List adjacent markets that are commonly confused with it.

3) Demand: Who Buys and Why Now
- Top buyer segments and jobs-to-be-done.
- 3–5 triggers that cause purchase.
- Evidence and proxies you used (search trends, hiring, spend categories, regulation, etc.).

4) Supply/Competition Map
- Identify major competitor types (incumbents, specialists, DIY, substitutes).
- Provide a 2x2 positioning map (axes you choose) and place archetypes on it.
- Call out likely non-obvious substitutes.

5) Economics Snapshot
- Typical pricing models and rough price bands.
- Unit economics drivers (CAC, payback, gross margin levers) and what would need to be true.

6) Adoption Friction and Risks
- Top 7 blockers (technical, organizational, regulatory, switching costs, trust).
- A pre-mortem: “If this fails, it’s because…” with probabilities.

7) Wedge and Differentiation Options
- 3 plausible entry wedges with rationale.
- For each: required capabilities, time-to-value, and why incumbents won’t copy quickly.

8) Evidence Table
- A table with columns: Claim | Confidence (High/Med/Low) | Evidence needed | How to get it fast | Notes.

9) 30-Day Validation Plan
- 10 specific tests (customer interviews, landing page, pricing tests, data collection, concierge pilot).
- For each: method, sample size target, success metric, decision rule, and expected cost/time.

10) Sources
- Provide links and citations for key numbers and claims. If you can’t browse, list the exact sources to check (publisher + report/database + what to extract).

Formatting rules
- Use short paragraphs and tables where useful.
- Quantify whenever possible; otherwise provide ranges and explain.
- End with: “What I would do next” (5 bullets) tailored to my context.

title: Turn One Blog Post Into 10 Platform-Specific Social Media Posts (Concise, Professional)
content:
I’m sharing a piece of content and I need ready-to-post social copy.

Source content (paste):
[PASTE BLOG/ARTICLE/THREAD/NOTES]

Target audience:
[WHO IT’S FOR]

Primary goal (pick one): awareness / clicks / leads / saves / replies

Platforms (pick any): LinkedIn, X, Instagram caption, TikTok caption, Facebook, Threads

Write 10 posts total, optimized for the selected platforms. Keep the tone professional and concise.

Requirements:
- For each post, include: Platform + Post text + 3–6 relevant hashtags (if appropriate for that platform).
- Vary the angle each time (e.g., insight, contrarian take, quick tip, mini-case study, myth vs fact, checklist, mistake to avoid, lesson learned, quote, question).
- Use plain language. No hype. No emojis unless the platform typically expects them.
- Include one clear call-to-action per post (comment, save, share, click, DM, etc.).
- If the source content is too long, extract the 5–7 most shareable points first, then write from those.

Output format:
Post 1:
Platform:
Text:
Hashtags:

…through Post 10.

title: Turn Product Notes Into a High-Converting Landing Page (with A/B variants)
content:
Act like a conversion-focused marketing copywriter. Using the inputs below, write landing page copy that is clear, specific, and benefits-led (friendly, helpful tone—no hype).

Inputs:
- Product/service:
- Ideal customer:
- Customer’s top pain points (bullets):
- Primary promise/outcome:
- Key features (bullets):
- Proof assets (testimonials, stats, logos, case studies):
- Objections/risks customers mention:
- Competitors/alternatives:
- Pricing model (if relevant):
- Voice constraints (words to avoid, brand personality):

Deliverables (keep each section skimmable):
1) Above-the-fold block
   - 8 headline options (mix of benefit-led, problem-led, outcome-led)
   - 8 subheadline options (clarify who it’s for + why now)
   - 6 CTA button options (varied intent: demo, trial, download, talk to sales)

2) Full landing page draft (in this order)
   - Hero (headline + subhead + primary CTA + secondary CTA)
   - “For who / not for who” (2 short lists)
   - Problem → impact → why current solutions fail (short, specific)
   - Solution overview (how it works in 3 steps)
   - Benefits (5–7 bullets) mapped to pains
   - Feature-to-benefit table (at least 6 rows)
   - Social proof section (write placeholders if none provided)
   - Objection handling (answer top 5 objections)
   - Pricing/plan framing (if pricing unknown, suggest 2–3 packaging options)
   - FAQ (6–8 questions)
   - Closing CTA block (restate outcome + risk reducer)

3) A/B test pack
   - 3 positioning angles (e.g., speed, reliability, cost, status/identity)
   - For each angle: headline + subhead + primary CTA + 2 proof ideas
   - 5 experiment ideas beyond copy (layout, proof placement, CTA style)

Rules:
- Avoid vague claims (“best”, “revolutionary”). Replace with concrete specifics.
- If any critical input is missing, make reasonable assumptions and label them as assumptions at the top.
- Keep reading level around grade 7–9.
- Use short paragraphs and bullets; no walls of text.

title: Hero image for a B2B email newsletter (confident, clean, grid theme)
content:
Create a modern hero image for a B2B growth newsletter email.

Subject & concept: An abstract “growth grid” visual—an isometric grid of thin white lines forming a subtle 3D plane, with a few highlighted nodes connected by minimalist lines, implying momentum and clarity (no charts with numbers).

Composition: Wide banner crop (recommended 1200×600). Clear safe area in the center-right for headline text overlay (leave it visually quiet). Primary focal point on the left third.

Style: Minimal, confident, structured, tech-forward. Crisp geometry, no clutter. No people, no devices, no logos, no readable text.

Color palette: Deep navy background (#0B1020) with accents in electric teal (#2DE2E6) and soft lime (#B8FF4A). Use subtle gradients, not neon bloom.

Lighting & texture: Soft studio lighting, gentle vignette, faint grain. Nodes should have a restrained glow and slight depth (embossed or glass-like spheres), lines razor-sharp.

Camera & rendering: Isometric angle (~30–35° tilt), high detail, clean edges, premium 3D/CG render look (Octane/Redshift vibe), sharp but not overly glossy.

Negative prompts: Avoid busy dashboards, stock-photo vibe, exaggerated lens flares, heavy bokeh, cartoon style, visible UI text, brand marks, and harsh noise.

Deliverable: Single image, email-safe hero banner, high contrast, ample whitespace for copy.

title: Create a 90-Day Growth Plan with Weekly Experiments (B2B or B2C)
content:
Act like a pragmatic growth lead. Build a 90-day growth plan for my business with weekly experiments I can actually run.

Business context:
- What we sell:
- Target customer:
- Price point / contract size:
- Current monthly traffic + top channels:
- Current conversion rates (visit→lead, lead→trial/demo, trial→paid):
- Sales cycle length:
- Main constraint (time, budget, team, product, data):
- What’s already been tried:
- North Star metric:

Output requirements (be specific, no fluff):
1) Growth model (1 page max):
   - The funnel stages that matter for this business
   - 3–5 leading indicators I should track weekly
   - The single biggest bottleneck you suspect and why

2) KPI targets for 90 days:
   - Give a realistic baseline→target for each KPI
   - Include assumptions behind the math (keep it simple)

3) Weekly experiment roadmap (12 weeks):
   - 2 experiments per week (24 total)
   - For each experiment include:
     - Hypothesis
     - Segment / audience
     - Channel + tactic
     - What to build/change (exact deliverable)
     - Success metric + threshold
     - Effort level (S/M/L) and expected impact (Low/Med/High)
     - How to run it in 3–5 steps
     - What could make results misleading

4) Channel mix:
   - Choose 3 “primary” channels and 2 “secondary” channels
   - Explain why they fit this business and constraint

5) Messaging:
   - 1 positioning statement
   - 5 headline/value-prop variations
   - 10 ad angles or outreach hooks (short, punchy)

6) Ops + cadence:
   - A weekly ritual (30–60 min) for reviewing results and deciding next actions
   - A simple experiment log template (table)

Rules:
- Prefer fast, testable actions over big projects.
- If information is missing, make reasonable assumptions and list them.
- Keep everything in clear tables/bullets so I can copy it into Notion.

Start by asking up to 5 clarifying questions only if you truly need them; otherwise proceed with assumptions.

title: Quick UI Critique + Actionable Fixes (Accessibility, UX, Visual Hierarchy)
content:
Act as a senior product designer. I’m going to paste a UI description (or screenshot text) below. Give a quick, practical critique and a short list of fixes I can implement today.

UI/context:
- Product + screen: 
- Primary user goal on this screen: 
- Platform (web/iOS/Android): 
- Key constraints (brand, dev limits, deadline): 

What I need from you:
1) Biggest 3 usability issues (in plain language) and why they matter.
2) Visual hierarchy check: what should be the #1 focal point, what currently is, and how to correct it.
3) Accessibility pass: color contrast, text sizing, focus states/keyboard nav, tap targets (call out likely WCAG pitfalls without being preachy).
4) Microcopy improvements: rewrite labels/buttons/errors (max 6 rewrites) to be clearer and more consistent.
5) “Do this first” plan: 5 bullet-point changes ordered by impact vs effort.

Keep it concise. If details are missing, make reasonable assumptions and list them under “Assumptions.”

Here’s the UI description:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Campaign Ops System: UTM schema, lead routing, and attribution rules (copy/paste)
content:
Act as a campaign-ops architect. Build a reusable growth system that standardizes tracking, attribution, and lead routing across paid + owned channels.

Context
- Company/product: [brief description]
- ICPs: [list]
- Markets/regions: [list]
- Primary conversion events: [e.g., demo request, trial start, purchase]
- CRM: [HubSpot/Salesforce/other]
- Ad platforms: [Google, Meta, LinkedIn, etc.]
- Analytics: [GA4/Amplitude/Mixpanel/other]
- Data warehouse (optional): [BigQuery/Snowflake/none]
- Routing constraints: [territories, segments, SLAs, round robin, named accounts]

Deliverables (return in the exact structure below)

1) Canonical Naming + UTM/Parameter Spec
- Provide a table with:
  - Field name (utm_source, utm_medium, utm_campaign, utm_content, utm_term, plus any custom params like gclid/fbclid/msclkid, campaign_id, adset_id, ad_id, creative_id, placement)
  - Allowed values / controlled vocabulary
  - Format rules (case, separators, max length)
  - Example
- Include a campaign naming convention formula that encodes: channel, objective, ICP/segment, geo, offer, quarter, and experiment flag.
- Add validation rules that can be enforced at link-creation time.

2) Channel Mapping Matrix
- Create a matrix mapping common sources/mediums to standardized channel groupings.
- Include rules to normalize messy inputs (e.g., “paid_social” vs “paidsocial”, “linkedin.com” vs “linkedin”, email vendors, affiliates, partner referrals).
- Output as a simple, machine-friendly table.

3) Attribution Model Rules (Operational)
- Define how to calculate:
  - First-touch
  - Last-touch
  - Lead-creation touch
  - Opportunity-creation touch
  - Revenue touch
- Specify lookback windows per channel (paid search, paid social, email, direct/organic).
- Provide tie-breakers when multiple touches qualify.
- State how you will handle:
  - Cross-domain tracking
  - Cookie loss / ITP
  - Offline conversions (calls, events)
  - Self-referrals and payment providers

4) Lead Routing Logic
- Produce routing rules in a decision-tree format (if/then) that assigns:
  - Owner (AE/SDR/CS)
  - Pipeline (inbound, self-serve, partner, expansion)
  - Priority score (P0/P1/P2)
  - SLA (time-to-first-touch)
- Inputs available: form fields, geo, employee size, industry, intent score, product interest, existing account match, partner code.
- Include a fallback path for missing/invalid data.

5) Data Contract (Events + Properties)
- Define a minimal event schema for the funnel with:
  - Event names (page_view, form_submit, signup_start, signup_complete, demo_request, trial_start, purchase, etc.)
  - Required properties (user_id, anonymous_id, timestamp, url, referrer, utm fields, campaign_id, device, geo)
  - Identity stitching rules
- Output in JSON Schema-like format.

6) QA Checklist + Monitoring
- Provide a pre-launch checklist (links, UTMs, pixel firing, form mapping, CRM fields).
- Provide post-launch monitoring queries/alerts described in plain steps (e.g., “alert if >10% of leads have null utm_campaign”, “spike in direct traffic”).
- Include a weekly ops review template that produces metrics, not prose.

7) Example Implementation Pack
- Give:
  - 5 example campaign names + UTMs for different channels
  - 3 example routing outcomes with sample lead payloads
  - A short “Do/Don’t” list for marketers creating links

Constraints
- Keep it platform-agnostic.
- Prefer deterministic rules over vague guidance.
- Use concise language; tables and structured blocks over paragraphs.

Ask up to 5 clarifying questions only if critical information is missing; otherwise make reasonable assumptions and state them briefly at the top.
